author: @allanhitchmoth3099
text:
I can't believe I missed this video until now.
For years I've felt that central generation was actually a terrible idea, at lest, being implemented as an exclusive method.
People become completely dependent and governments even pass laws that REQUIRE grid-connected services or, one is accused of such things as "child endangerment" and the like!

Imagine the boon to human welfare if just 10% of the private homes were "off-grid" capable, even if only for 30 days!

In times of emergency, this would equate to (at least) 10% fewer families that were in peril. This of course, also means that there would be 10% fewer events to endanger first responders! 
Not to mention the assistance that could be rendered to others in an affected neighborhood.

I honestly believe it could even be argued that such individualized/localized capability, even in the short term, would be of great benefit to national security.

The very idea, however, of benevolent individualism is an all but lost philosophy, I fear.

Thank you for your outstanding videos!

author: @mikehogan1827
text:
Buckets of sand - priceless!
Power stations requiring 25-40 years?  Certainly, conventional hydro and nuclear can take a long time. But natural gas fired plants can be permitted and built in as little as 3 years, in Canada.  5-6 years is not a problem. 
It’s arguable that the reason why central plant was “right” was because of (a) economies of scale, and (b) diversity inherent in large aggregated loads. But over the years, the costs of central generation have increased dramatically, while the costs of distributed generation have actually declined - largely thanks to materials science and mass production. 
And competition, of course. Central utilities haven’t improved their cost management nearly as much as small generation manufacturers have reduced their products’ costs. 
Also, the costs of transmission and distribution have increased dramatically. Remember, the centralized model requires the consumer to pay the total cost of generation plus transmission plus distribution.  That total cost is what micro (or “behind the fence”) generators are competing against.

author: @JA-ti3wd
text: I generally agree with what you say with 2 exceptions. Firstly, civilian nuclear power is designed with layers of back ups so the idea of 1 mistake resulting in a full on disaster isn't realistic in the UK. The layers of protection, verification, independent analysis, oversight and periodic reviews make the likelihood of a single error causing a major incident vanishingly unlikely. Secondly I seriously doubt you will ever get away from a centralised electrical model for this country. If you want to make sure the same appliance can be used at 1 end of the country as the other without the need for adaptors or transformers, a single grid system guarantees that. Yes, you can have an event that takes down the whole grid but the overall likelihood of prolonged black outs is greater with small grids. That's not to preclude a large expansion of small scale generation being added to the system, I'm just arguing it should be a single network.

text: I have a problem that I need help with. I have a small 800+sq ft home in the midwest USA.  I have insulated my house, attic, basement sill, and walls. My heating system consists of an antique gravity-feed coal burner converted to gas in the basement. The burner heats up an iron plate and gravity does the rest. I don't have the kind of duct work that modern homes do, this thing looks like an octopus with big round arms connecting to vents located down the center wall of the house. You might guess that it is not as efficient as a modern central air system and I don't have central air conditioning, however, it's not that bad. I wouldn't save enough in energy bills to justify replacing the system, so I haven't. In addition, replacing the ductwork would cost thousands since it would have to be completely redone, and the old stuff is taped with asbestos tape. What can I do with it to make it more efficient myself?  I've thought about adding a mini-split to the existing system, but not sure that it would save me anything since that is electric. Note that my current system has been used since the house was built in 1936 (not sure when it was converted to gas, but perhaps 60 - 70 years?). The only maintenace I've done is replace the heating coil. There are no moving parts.

author: @Reman1975
text:
Back in the "50p electric meter" days we had much more of a mental connection to our electricity use. If you left an electric heater on 24/7, you'd have to put more coins in the meter because of it. You had a much more immediate cause and effect loop. So maybe the way to make people more aware of their energy use would be to have a little display next to their thermostat that shows what power they're using in real time? It could be connected to the electric and gas smart meters and show how much they using there and then, how this compares to their average use, or the average use around that time for the last week, cost per hour at their current tariff, a running total of roughly what their next bill will be, stuff like that. If it had an option to allow you to view logs as graphs (Complete with red peaks to indicate when you've used more than your average at that time) it would help folks

It would probably have more of a phycological effect than just getting an eye-watering trimonthly bill, and could be a useful tool for people to use so they can actually make informed choices. For example, you'd SEE the cost difference as it was happening between running a dishwasher cycle Vs heating the water to wash the stuff by hand. By turning them off for a few minutes, you could gauge how much power your old freezer or plasma TV uses, and work out if a more energy efficient new one may be worth the cost. Or you could see in real time how much money you can save by fully turning off all those devices in your home that have a "Standby" parasitic power draw.

Several years ago I bought one of those power meters that you plug into the wall socket, then plug your device into so you can see how much mains power it was drawing (I actually got it so I could work out what power hungry equipment I could simultaneously have running in the garage without the constant trips to the fuse box to reset breakers in the house. :D). I'm a bit of a nerd, and have multiple computers dotted around the house, so out of curiosity one day, I connected my gaming computer, it's monitors, speakers, and all it's other mains powered bit's and bobs, all into a single power strip, and through this meter to see what amount of power it pulled. The only answer I remember is "more than I'd thought !". The thing is, after I turned it back off, and everything went into standby, I noticed that it was still pulling power at a rate that was again "More than I'd thought". I tested a couple other PC's around the place and all of them had a similar standby draw.

So, a little bit of maths later, I worked out that if I bought couple of budget multi gang trailing sockets that had a switch on them, plus use a handful of others I've already got that have switches in better thought out locations, then shut them off when those PC weren't in use, at the (Then) price of electricity, the new power strips would only need to last for (IIRC) 12 years before they'd paid for themselves. With the current jump in electric prices it's probably a fair bit less than that now.

"Why not turn them off at the sockets?" I hear you all cry. The sockets are nearly all behind desks, cupboards, and TV stands, so it's aggro getting to them, and we all know, that if you need to crawl under a desk, or move furniture to get to the switch, it doesn't religiously get switched. The trailing sockets are all now positioned to be in easy reach. The desktop ones are actually velcroed to the back top corner of the desks so the wires all hang down out the way behind them, so I don't really have to expend much effort to flick the switches off after the PC's are shut down.

Anyway, The point is, without seeing that information, I wouldn't have realised exactly how much these computers were using (plus a few other devices around the house that go into standby, but weren't essential that they stayed powered). Granted, It wasn't a huge amount, but it was still enough that in 12 years I'd essentially have a couple more power strips for free, and after that, I'd save enough to go into Starbucks for a coffee every 3 or 4 years. :D . So the concept of having an easily read, "Energy meter" in the home so I could see what's being used is quite appealing.

author: @TonyGingrich
text:
Nail on the head! I would go a step further... Electricity is not the only power source capable of doing work. Many of our most energy-demanding appliances solve problems by converting electrical energy into mechanical energy. Washing machines, fans, blowers, vacuums, garage doors, etc... My thinking is that the best way to reduce electrical demand is to source energy from old-school methods.

Windmills and water wheels can be used to provide a central mechanical power source. For a home or an entire community. Energy could be tapped by way of pneumatics, hydraulics, or axles/gears. Anything that typically uses an electric motor, could instead be driven by the tapped mechanical source.

Once you've culled your electrical demand down to mostly solid-state appliances, you can go further. Most of those have transformers built in, to step the AC line voltage down to a lower volt DC current. Meaning: those appliances do not inherently require line service. Remove the transformers, and serve a ready-made DC current directly to those. Use voltage dividers where necessary.

Another note... transformers are very inefficient electrical components. With no load applied, the field winding continues to spend energy. So even though the appliance still requires the same power on its side; eliminating the transformer, alone, reduces the cummilative demand.

author: @chrisbaker3066
text:
I have been off the grid in Australia for the past 33 years. I gather my own water, dispose of sewage, produce my own power and grow what food I can using hydroponics. I live in a climate with cold winters and moderate summers but with minus 6 frosts but mostly sunny winter days, solar iss ideal here. However, I live alone now and and unless you want to spend a fortune to set yourself up with a massive system, you have to modify how you live. I contend that many people will be forced to adopt a much more basic lifestyle than I endure, when the system grows too overloaded by demand or the economy tanks. It happened in my fathers lifetime 3 times. His expectations were not excessive and his acceptance for his own labour input into his life was great. That is how my people survived for generations and I try to emulate that stoicism.
My costs for power are around $450 a year when I have to replace the panels and batteries. Many people have solar panels on their roofs but storage is the issue. Our transmission system cannot cope with the extra power produced from the solar farms and urban roofs springing up all over the countryside. The privatised power companies are rebuilding transmission structures but guess who will pay for it? Government predicts a 50 % increase on top of  already expensive bills.

Because  people don't want to lose the convenience of huge modern powered houses, they talk about huge cost solar batteries ( Tesla Powerwall is $ 17k here in OZ) They fear lead acid batteries for instance ,which don't catch fire and are a proven technology ( I get 10 tears plus from mine) because they can't use a hydrometer! Instead they continue to pay for the convenience of a mains supply with all the issues that you have mentioned.

In the end people have willingly yoked themselves to this system and  it is up to those people to take responsibility for their own lives. However, I fear that it is too late for most.
I love your channel and.hope that you continue to produce this good content. I am tinkering with a number of your ideas at the moment.Sorry for the novella!!

author: @davidsoulsby1102
text:
A point or 2 of clarification on Big or Small.
Big generation plants are not more venerable to storms, your example is not an issues with the generation type but the distribution.
These power cuts are not because of damage to the generation or the national grid distribution network as they are well over engineered to withstand hurricane force storms,  add to that the network is designed to be switched around any faulted area in anywhere from 5 mins to 30 mins and restore everyone.
The network operators system which is fed from the national grid is similar to the national grid but there are masses more of it and its in a much smaller geographical area. When a storm comes it may hit 2%-5% of the national network but it will hit 50% -100% of the local network. This causes multiple faults on the local network and swamps all the systems put in place, it also wipes out the available staff to fault ratio. one national network fault may take at most a dozen staff to actually fix. a distribution may only take 4 - 6 men but you will have 100 and more faults to deal with.
Around this time last year the North, North East and North Yorkshire. Northern Powergrid's area had over 20000 individual reports, which ended up in over 4000 actual physical network repairs. it took less than 16hrs to do the damage but 10 days to temp restore supplies and another 3 weeks to carry out full repairs, that was with every engineer that could be spared for the whole of the British Isles.
The worst storm in my 40 years....
there is regular storms of about half that intensity every couple of years.
So small guarantees nothing when it comes to loss of supply unless.............. you generate it yourself.

author: @petersloper9674
text:
Rob’s being uncharacteristically wooly in his analysis here. I think no one denies there is "any problem at all”. But some people insist on using cause and effect to pinpoint what that problem is in order to arrive at accurate and workable solutions to it (and thereby to its knock-on effects further down the line).  If we “look at the population growth” and the corresponding increased “energy demand” which is the inevitable effect arising from it, then it seems to me the problem we face can be identified and named as “population growth” and whatever has caused that growth to come about. It may well be its cause is exclusively political - in which case a political solution is likely to be far more effective in resolving the problem of increased energy demand than anything which misidentifies (or avoids) its true cause. Furthermore (although Rob fails to mention the fact) Britain is sitting atop 400 year’s worth of coal which can be relatively cleanly burnt using modern technology to generate cheap and abundant energy. The fact that we are not already doing so again points to the problem being political in its root cause. 

If Rob wants to travel down this path with realistic analysis and outcomes, he cannot do so convincingly without including politics in his overviews. If that’s not his bag, then maybe he should steer well clear of the issues and concentrate on building stuff to help us mitigate the problems caused by a source others can more readily identify and name.

author: @totherarf
text:
You are as per usual right about your conclusions here.
However, working in the Electricity Supply Industry I think a couple of corrections are in order, if I may?

You say we are using vastly more power than we used to back in the 70's ..... I understand where you get that idea from, but it is exaggerated. 
This will involve some Big figures, but here goes .... Back in 1970 we used roughly 240TWh but by 2015 we had only gone up to 310TWh .....  roughly a quarter in the best part of 50 years!
This is mainly due to a change in our economy from "Making Big Stuff" to smaller manufacture and building devices from imported parts. 

The next point is the Importing of power. We have done this for quite some time (and we export it too)! You say 60% but that is only if you include Gas and Oil, which I think are outside this discussion. Electricity imports alter year by year up and down but hit a recent low in 2010 of 7,144 MWh and has increased up to 2,8743 MWh last year. Figures are not in yet but we have been Exporting Electricity to Europe this winter to help alleviate energy deficits there due to recent conflicts! 
So a ,ore accurate figure might be a couple of % Import / Generated output!

Generation of Electricity is only one side of things though ..... the other is Distribution!
Back pre WW2 we had a centralised regional distribution system. The country was divided into 12 Regional Electricity Councils (Scottish Power, Eastern, NORWEB, MANWEB ... et al) Each of which had a distribution grid that only supplied people within their own area, but supplied All those within. A weakness was seen in that each area could only supply and be supplied within itself so a "Grid" was set up so that power could be shared from all the generators to all of the different distribution main nodes (Primary Substations). Hence we saw pylons girdling the country. This means that a Control Engineer can call for X amount of power from a power station, be it Gas, Nuclear or Hydro) and know the system would balance. Indeed this is why we have power at 50Hz to within a % point.

OK now that is done with we can look at the "Solution" ..... Distributed Generation! 

All is not well with this, although many may not be aware!

One problem is the method of providing that power. If it is within your home all is well, but some want to export there excess to the grid. This means they need to have a higher voltage (slightly) than the mains, otherwise the power would not flow in the reverse direction. This voltage is usually generated from an inverter. Now inverters are a fine and dandy thing (as someone is want to say) But it is Not the same waveform as the mains .... close but not the same! there is a superimposed stepped voltage that can cause not only higher voltages than are allowed but can cause a Harmonic "ringing" ...... usually 7th and 13th harmonics (this is getting too technical but trust me, there are problems ..... especially with cheaper inverters!
The next problem is that the supply system is finely balanced with voltages to be supplied within a certain range. If you have a nice estate with PV's on every other roof and you get a nice sunny day the voltage goes up. And when those clouds come it goes down. How is anybody supposed to supply energy with that kind of external influence? Further all those EV car chargers switch off and need to be reset every time the voltage hits 257. This means complaints to the DNO that are difficult to sort and not caused by the DNO!

replies.author: @jb-dn3ct
replies.text: You do have some good points, my understanding of electrics is limited as I'm in no way an electrical engineer or work in anyway relating with electricity (other than experimenting with things I learn from others) but I always thought power invertors changed dc to ac and vice versa, I thought transformers were what's  used to step voltage up or down?

replies.author: @totherarf
replies.text:
@jb-dn3ct Yes you are right! You can also have current transformers that will give a current based transform  ..... you see them on big metering and they are dangerous if you don't know what you are doing. 
The inverters give an analogue of a sine wave, but it has lots of steps in the voltage (more steps the more expensive but the closer the analogue to a sine wave) it is the number of steps and the voltage of each step that sets up a resonance or Harmonic. 
I think that is more info than most have on it but I don't want to swamp you with tech or math ;o) ..... and it has been 40 odd years since I was learning this stuff! 
There is plenty of stuff online if you want to take a dive in there though and a couple of min video can get as much info over as a whole book!

replies.text:
6:32 That's a little disingenuous. Old nuclear plants (like Chernobyl) had issues. Newer plants have mostly fixed those. IF something were to happen, the radioactive stuff drains out into a holding tank and with nothing to force the reaction, it cools by itself. Completely passive requiring no input from us pesky humans. No risk of meltdown. No risk of explosion. Is it perfect? No. The liquid salts required for this particular type of reactor are very corrosive, which requires either new materials, or constant repair. No system is perfect. But some systems are far better than others.

The fact that you can name every disaster (there's only 3, and only one was really bad) that has happened with Nuclear since its inception nearly 80 years ago when it supplies 10% of worldwide power should tell you that it's reliable and relatively safe. The cost in lives from Solar and Wind is much higher than Nuclear yet we seem to have no issues pushing those technologies...

When I was pre-five years, we lived in a farmhouse, with no electricity, no running water, single glazing etc. In the winter it was pretty chilly. Tilley and oil lamps, paraffin heaters a coal fire and a Rayburn stove provided all the heat, light and cooking facilities. The Grid was not important at all.
My parents kept goats and bantams, for milk and cheese and eggs. Rabbits and pigeons were a regular meat and along with home grown vegetables, we didn’t starve.
The Grid, has essentially, been imposed upon us. It is a conditioned reliance, which we could do without. 
Do I believe there is a shortage of energy, when the World’s data centres, are consuming over 250 terrawatt hours? No!
This country has had it’s natural resources sold off. Our oil and gas is owned by foreign corporations and sold back to us, at overly escalated prices. 
There are no mistakes. This is deliberate built in obsolescence, in a system, where the majority suffer the most.

author: @tradermunky1998

This is actually what Tesla is working on, decentralization of the entire electrical grid.

Everyone would still be connected to a centralized grid but have their own generation (through solar) and perhaps battery backup. Each person would then become mini power plants buying and selling electricity to and fro. So if I'm not home, but my neighbor is, software would match my excess power sell to their excess buy/need and I would sell my electricity to them with the electric company acting much like a stock market exchange company taking a small piece of the profit.

Centralized electric generation and delivery would only be provided only as needed.

Electric companies are wanting to move forward to this as they provide mostly just a grid for delivery and a middle man for brokerage while getting out of the electric generation mostly. Much easier, more money for them, we all take up the upkeep of our mini power plant.

author: @misamsung6191
text: I find it interesting in reading the comments that most people do not understand how electricity works beyond throwing a switch or pushing a button.  Microgen isn't a new concept in the early days of electrification there were many small communities that rolled their own power.  The problem was that they couldn't be interconnected because of the differences in voltage, frequency and phase of their electricity.  Not to mention that as electricity became more consumer driven there needed to be a commonality between the various producers.  Which led to the larger generating stations, economy of scale and economy of standardisation. Today it is possible to do Microgen and match the voltage, frequency and phase with the main grid.  But that equipment will add to the cost of Microgen.  If one wishes to be off grid then all that would be required is for their genset to produce the same voltage and frequency as the main grid.
